When a layer has Mesh mode enabled, fills can be assigned to different sides of the mesh:

  • Fill appears on both sides
  • Fill appears only on front side
  • Fill appears only on back side

items.title: Removing a Mesh
items.text:
To remove a mesh from a layer:

  1. Select the layer with the active mesh
  2. Either:
  3. Navigate to Layer → Mesh → Remove
  4. Click the mesh icon in the layer row

For detailed information about working with meshes, see the dedicated Mesh chapter.

items.title: Removing the layer
items.text: There are several ways to remove a layer from your document when you no longer need it.

items.title: Using the Layers Panel
items.text:
The quickest way to remove a layer is through the Layers panel:

  1. Select the layer you want to remove by clicking on it in the Layers panel
  2. Click the Delete button at the bottom of the Layers panel

items.title: Using Keyboard Shortcuts
items.text:
You can also remove a selected layer using keyboard shortcuts:

  • Press {Del} or {Backspace} key after selecting the layer

items.title: Using the Menu
items.text:
Another option is to use the menu:

  1. Select the layer you want to remove
  2. Go to Layer > Delete

When you delete a layer, all fills and masks within that layer will also be deleted. This action cannot be undone after you save the document, so be careful when removing layers.

items.title: What Happens When You Remove a Layer
items.text:
When you remove a layer:

  • All fills contained in that layer are permanently removed
  • The layer's mask is deleted
  • If the layer was in Mesh mode, all mesh settings are lost
  • Any visual effects from that layer disappear from your artwork

items.title: Tips for Managing Layers
items.text:
Instead of deleting layers you're unsure about, consider:

  • Hiding layers - Click the eye icon to hide a layer instead of deleting it
  • Locking layers - Use the lock icon to prevent accidental changes
  • Creating groups - Organize related layers into groups for better management
  • Renaming layers - Give descriptive names to layers so you know their purpose

Sometimes it's better to hide a layer temporarily rather than delete it, especially when experimenting with different design approaches.

items.title: Adding mask
items.text: A mask determines which parts of your fill are visible. Think of it as a stencil that controls where your artwork appears. You can add a mask to any layer, giving you precise control over your design.

items.title: Adding a Mask
items.text:
To create and edit masks in Vexy Lines, you can use specialized tools such as the Brush, Rectangle, Ellipse, and Freeform. These tools allow you to craft a mask outline around the desired area.

Select a layer in the Layers panel and use any of these tools from the toolbar:

Brush {B}: Paint your mask freehand - Click and drag to paint white areas (showing your fill) - Hold {⌥} while painting to create black areas (hiding your fill) - Adjust brush size using the toolbar controls or hold {⌃} and drag - Use a small brush for detailed work and a large brush for covering broader areas

Rectangle {I} Create rectangular masks - Click and drag to draw a rectangular mask - Hold {⇧} while dragging to create a perfect square - Single-click on an object to automatically detect and mask its rectangular shape - Hold {⇧} while clicking multiple areas to add to your mask - Hold {⌥} while clicking to remove areas from your existing mask

Ellipse {O}: Draw circular masks - Click and drag to create an elliptical mask - Hold {⇧} while dragging for a perfect circle - Single-click on a circular object to auto-detect its shape - Combine with {⇧} or {⌥} for adding to or subtracting from your mask

Freeform {S}: Create custom mask shapes - Click points to create a custom shape - Close the shape to complete your mask - Single-click on complex objects to let Vexy Lines detect their shape - Use with {⇧} or {⌥} modifiers to add or subtract from existing masks

When you add a mask, it appears next to the layer in the Layers panel. White areas of the mask show your fill, while black areas hide it.

For more details on using these mask drawing tools, refer to the following articles: Brush, Rectangle, Ellipse, Freeform.

items.title: Tips for Working with Masks
items.text:
• Toggle fill visibility: Turn off your fill visibility for clearer mask editing
  • Use the View controls: Adjust edge highlighting to better see your mask boundaries
  • Combine tools: Use different mask tools in combination for the best results
  • Save versions: When trying complex masking techniques, save different versions of your file

items.title: Importing mask
items.text:
Easily import a pre-designed mask using an SVG file.

To do so, follow these steps: 1. Navigate to File -> Import -> Artwork from the menu. 2. In the dialog box that appears, choose the SVG file you wish to import. 3. Then, decide on the action you'd like to take:

If a specific Layer is selected: * Replace Mask: This will substitute the mask in the currently selected Layer. * Add Mask: This will append a new mask to the chosen Layer.

If no Layer is selected, you'll be given the option to create a new layer that includes the imported mask.

If you want the mask to match the document's dimensions, ensure the Scale to document option is checked.

Following these steps will either add a new layer with the imported mask to your document or integrate the mask into an existing layer.

items.title: Mask and mesh
items.text:
If layer has a mesh, the mask within this Layer gains an additional Pinned property.

This feature is designed to "pin" the mask to the mesh. When in the Pinned state, all standard transformation operations (move, rotate, resize) applied to the mesh will also be applied to the mask.

pinned mask rotate shear

$~$

unpinned mask rotate shear

You can enable or disable this property from the Layers panel. To the right of the Mesh object, an icon appears. By clicking on this icon, you can toggle the Pinned property on and off.

items.title: Mask editing 1
items.text:
You can easily modify the mask on your chosen Layer. Use tools like the Brush, Rectangle, Ellipse, and Freeform to draw new sections or erase unwanted areas.

For more precise edits, utilize the Editor tool.

Start by selecting a curve on the mask. You can choose multiple curves by holding down the {⇧} key while clicking. Once selected, you can drag individual or multiple points to adjust their positions.

Dive deeper into the Editor tool's capabilities in the Editor article.

items.title: Add and Remove Points
items.text:
To insert points, either use the Knife tool or simply click on the desired curve spot with the Editor tool while holding the {⌥} key.

To remove points and segments, select and delete specific curves using the {Backspace} or {Del} keys.

To remove the whole mask, delete all of its curves. Doing so will apply fills to the entire document area.

items.title: Inverting Mask
items.text: !!!2 Для инвертирования маски используйте меню Layer -> Invert Mask | mask: normal | mask: inverted| | --- | --- | |||

items.title: Overlay
items.text:
In Vexy Lines, each layer has an Overlay option. When turned on, the mask becomes solid, covering its selected area and hiding any masks below it. Essentially, a layer with the activated Overlay will be displayed on top of other underlying layers.

overlay: off overlay: on

The Overlay's behavior is also influenced by the group's Overlay setting:

  • If the group's Overlay is on, its masks will cover any layers below the group.
  • If it's off, the masks will only affect layers within that group.
group overlay: off group overlay: on group overlay: on

You can enable or disable the Overlay property in the Layers panel. The Overlay icon is located on the right side, next to the Group or Layer line.

items.title: Smooth Mask
items.text:
When activated, this mode enhances the mask with added transparency and softens the edges for a smoother appearance.

You can find this property and its settings in the "Smooth Mask" properties panel. For a detailed guide on how to use this feature, refer to the Smooth Mask article.

When this mode is activated, masking gains additional transparency and edge blurring properties.

This masking mode is based on raster masks. By default, this mode is turned off, and the vector masking mode is used, which provides a more precise result.

items.title: Opacity
items.text:
The Opacity property determines the degree of transparency of the mask in relation to other layers beneath it.

top layer opacity: 100% top layer opacity: 50% top layer opacity 50%, visibility off

items.title: Feather
items.text:
The Feather radius property determines the degree of blurriness of the mask's edge.

radius: 0 radius: 10 radius: 25

items.title: Modify
items.text: The Modify section provides tools to adjust your mask's shape and appearance after creation. These tools help you refine your mask without starting over, giving you more control over your artwork.

items.title: Expand or Contract
items.text: Make your entire mask larger or smaller: - Grow the mask outward (positive values) - Shrink the mask inward (negative values) - Fine-tune the mask's coverage

items.title: Edge to Stroke
items.text: Transform your mask's edge into a thick stroke: - Convert the boundary into a stroke - Adjust the stroke width as needed - Create interesting outline effects

items.title: Adding mesh
items.text:
Adding a mesh to your layer is the first step toward creating advanced transformations and effects in Vexy Lines. A mesh allows you to manipulate your artwork in ways that would be difficult or impossible with standard vector editing tools.

Before adding a mesh, make sure you have: - Created at least one layer with fills - Selected the layer you want to apply the mesh to - Considered which mesh shape will best suit your design goals

items.title: Adding Mesh
items.text:
1. Ensure a Layer is selected.

  2. In the Toolbar of the Layer panel, click the Set Mesh button.

  3. Choose a desired Mesh shape from the list.

As a result: - The selected shape will be applied to the chosen Layer. - A Mesh icon will appear in the Layer row, allowing you to toggle the Mesh mode on or off by clicking it.

items.title: Mesh Templates
items.text:
Vexy Lines offers several predefined mesh shapes, each suited for different artistic purposes:

Template Example Use Case   Rectangle Creating perspective in architectural designs   Donut Creating circular labels or badges   Rising wave Creating wave patterns or flowing fabrics   Falling wave Creating waterfall-like designs   Rising ribbon Creating spiraling or twisted shapes   Falling ribbon Creating descending spiral patterns

Once you've added a mesh to your layer:

  1. Your fills will be transformed according to the mesh's shape
  2. The mesh grid becomes visible when the layer is selected
  3. You can edit the mesh using the Editor tool
  4. Fills can be assigned to the front side, back side, or both sides of the mesh
  5. You can toggle the mesh on/off using the mesh icon in the layer row

items.title: Tips for Choosing the Right Mesh
items.text:
• Start simple: Begin with the Rectangle mesh for basic transformations
  • Match shape to purpose: Choose a mesh shape that closely resembles your desired final form
  • Consider complexity: More complex meshes (like ribbons) may require more editing
  • Plan ahead: Think about how you'll want to manipulate the mesh before selecting a type
  • Experiment: Don't hesitate to try different mesh types to see which works best

For more detailed information about working with meshes after adding them, see the dedicated Mesh chapter.

items.title: Mesh and mask
items.text:
When a layer has a Mesh, its mask comes with a Pinned option. This feature allows the mask to move in sync with the Mesh. When you adjust the Mesh by moving, rotating, or resizing it, the mask follows along accordingly.

In Mesh mode, the mask within this Layer gains an additional Pinned property. This feature is designed to "pin" the mask to the Mesh object. When in the Pinned state, all standard transformation operations (move, rotate, resize) applied to the Mesh will also be applied to the mask.

pinned mask rotate shear unpinned mask rotate shear

You can enable or disable this property from the Layers panel. To the right of the Mesh object, an icon appears. By clicking on this icon, you can toggle the Pinned property on and off.

items.title: Mesh editing
items.text: After adding a mesh to your layer, you can customize it to achieve your desired effect.

items.title: Basic Editing
items.text:
Use the Editor tool to begin editing your mesh. Click on any edge to make it active. Active edges show points available for editing.

Hold the {⇧} key to select multiple edges at once.

You can move individual points, select groups of points, move groups, and delete points as needed.

Use the transformation tool (⌘T) to transform selected edges by scaling, rotating, or reshaping them.

items.title: Adding Points
items.text: You can add points to mesh edges using three methods:

items.title: Using {⌥} Key
items.text:
Hold the {⌥} key and click where you want to add a point. On straight segments, this adds Bezier curve points. On curve segments, this adds a simple point at the clicked location.

Note: A point on an edge differs from a vertex. Vertices occur where horizontal and vertical edges intersect. Regular points can exist anywhere along an edge.

items.title: Using Quick Add Buttons
items.text: Click the {[+]} buttons that appear between points on your selected edge to add a new point between existing ones.

items.title: Using the Knife Tool
items.text:
For precise placement:

  1. Select the Knife tool from the tools panel
  2. Click on your edge where you want to add a point
  3. A new point will appear at the clicked location

items.title: Deleting Points
items.text:
To delete points, select them and press {Backspace} or {Del}. Only regular points can be deleted, not vertices where edges intersect.

Vexy Lines preserves the shape of curves when points are deleted.

items.title: Deleting Edges
items.text:
To delete an edge, select it and press {Backspace} or {Del}.

Important: Only added edges can be deleted. Original edges that came with the mesh cannot be removed.

items.title: Helpful Tips
items.text:
These techniques can improve your mesh editing workflow:

  • Hold {⌘}/{⌃} while dragging points for more precise movements
  • Add extra points in areas that need smoother curves
  • Use only as many points as necessary to achieve your desired shape
  • Save your work regularly when editing complex meshes

For more information about mesh properties and Hidden Strokes Removal, see the Mesh Properties article.

items.title: Mesh properties
items.text:
The MESH section of the Properties panel lets you adjust the mesh grid structure of your design in Strokes Maker, making it easier to fine-tune your artwork.

Think of a mesh as a flexible grid that you can reshape to transform your artwork. The Mesh Properties panel gives you control over this grid's structure and behavior. Here you can:

  1. Add more grid lines (edges) to increase control over specific areas
  2. Make your mesh behave like a real 3D object with the Hidden Strokes Removal feature
  3. Control which parts of your artwork are visible when the mesh folds over itself

On this panel, you can quickly add new horizontal or vertical edges to the Mesh grid, switch the object to Hidden Strokes Removal mode, and manage its options.

Learn more about the Mesh grid in this article Mesh.

items.title: Adding Edges
items.text:
Adding edges gives you more control points to reshape your mesh. More edges mean finer control over specific areas of your design.

When you select a mesh edge (a grid line), you can easily add new edges adjacent to it. For a vertical edge, you can add edges to the left or right. For a horizontal edge, you can add edges above or below the selected one.

To add an edge: 1. Select an edge in your mesh grid 2. In the Mesh Properties panel, click one of the edge addition buttons:

Add an edge at the top.

Add an edge at the bottom.

Add an edge on the right side.

Add an edge on the left side.

The examples below show how adding edges in different directions affects your mesh:

Add to the bottom Add to the top Add to the right Add to the left

items.title: When to Add Edges
items.text: Add more edges when you need: - More precise control over a specific area of your design - To create complex curves or bends in one part of your mesh - To add detail to just one section without affecting the entire mesh

items.title: Hidden Strokes Removal
items.text:
Activating this mode treats the mesh object like an opaque two-sided surface, similar to a sheet of paper. It hides fills that are covered by folds or intersections of the mesh surface.

This feature makes your mesh behave like a real physical object: - Parts that would be hidden by folds become invisible - Overlapping sections only show the top layer - The mesh acts like a solid object rather than a transparent grid

Think of it as folding a piece of paper - when you fold it, you can't see what's on the folded part that's facing away from you.

Hidden Strokes Removal Off Hidden Strokes Removal On

In the example above: - Left: All parts of the mesh are visible, even if they should be hidden by folds - Right: Parts of the mesh that would be hidden by folds are no longer visible

items.title: Removal Direction
items.text:
The lines on the mesh can be drawn in different sequences, and depending on this order, different parts of the mesh surface will be visible.

To set the drawing order, use the four buttons provided.

Direction determines which parts of your mesh are drawn "on top" when there's an overlap:

  • The drawing sequence defines which parts of the mesh are considered "in front"
  • Changing the direction can dramatically alter which parts of your artwork remain visible
  • This is similar to changing your viewing angle of a 3D object

items.title: Direction Options
items.text:
Top to down, left to right.

Bottom to up, right to left.

Left to right, top to bottom.

Right to left, top to bottom.

These examples show how different direction settings affect which parts of the mesh remain visible:

Top to down Bottom to up Left to right Right to left

items.title: Precision Settings
items.text:
You can adjust the accuracy of removing hidden parts of the fills. In most cases, there's no need to change this setting. However, if your design has tiny details or the mesh has many complex folds, you might need to increase the precision.

Note: Increasing precision can make the fill calculation take longer and use more memory.

Precision controls how accurately Vexy Lines calculates which parts should be hidden:

  • Low precision: Faster but may miss small details or create artifacts in complex areas
  • Higher precision: More accurate but requires more processing time
  • When to increase: If you see unwanted artifacts or incorrect hiding in detailed areas
Low Normal High Greatest

Notice how the higher precision settings show cleaner edges and more accurate hiding of overlapped areas.

items.title: Common Use Cases
items.text: Here are some practical applications for the Mesh Properties panel:

items.title: Adding Detail to Specific Areas
items.text: Add edges only where you need more control, leaving the rest of the mesh simpler: - Add edges near curves that need refinement - Create finer control in areas with important details - Keep edge count lower in simpler areas for easier editing

items.title: Creating Realistic Folded Effects
items.text: Use Hidden Strokes Removal to create paper-like folding effects: - Activate this mode when creating origami-inspired designs - Experiment with different removal directions to get the desired fold appearance - Increase precision for crisp fold lines in detailed work

items.title: Creating Depth and Perspective
items.text: Combine mesh editing with Hidden Strokes Removal to create depth: - Shape your mesh to create a 3D-like form - Enable Hidden Strokes Removal to make the depth convincing - Adjust direction settings to control which parts appear in front

items.title: Troubleshooting Common Issues
items.text:
If your mesh doesn't look right: - Try different removal directions if the wrong parts are hidden - Increase precision if you see glitches at intersection points - Add more edges if you need finer control over a specific area

For more information about editing mesh points and edges, see the Mesh Editing article.

items.title: Backup
items.text:
• Auto Save: Automatically saves the current document to prevent loss of work in case of unexpected interruptions or similar incidents. The document will be saved in a temporary file, which will be offered for recovery upon restarting the program.

  • Save Backup: This feature ensures that when you save a document, the previous version of that document is also saved. The backup file will have the same name as the original but will have an *.~lines extension. This provides an added layer of safety, allowing you to revert to the previous version if needed.

items.title: Colors
items.text:
• Fill: Adjust the highlight color for selected fills. This allows you to highlight the selected fills along their contour with the chosen color.
  • Mask: Set the highlight color for masks and the mesh grid.
  • Background: Set the color for the workspace background.

These options can also be found in the View panel.

Use the Reset button to restore values to their default settings.

items.title: Export 4
items.text:
The "Export Strokes as" option determines how strokes are exported to vector format: * Normal: Strokes are displayed as closed shapes with fill. * Scribble: Strokes are displayed as a simple zigzag line, evenly filling the fill line at a specified interval. The interval is determined by the number of vertical zigzag segments per 1mm of the fill line.

The SCRIBBLE mode can be useful if you intend to use the exported file for CNC machine output and similar purposes.

normal scribble

Scribble export is not applicable to Halftone, Traced, and Text fills.

items.title: Preview shortcut
items.text:
The Preview Shortcut is a keyboard combination that activates a clean preview mode. In this mode, all control elements, source images, and highlights are hidden, displaying only the rendered result.

Using {⌘ Space}/{⌃Space}, you can choose between two zoom actions:

  • Dynamic Zoom: Activated by pressing the key combination and dragging the mouse. Dragging to the right zooms in relative to the drag's starting point, while dragging to the left zooms out.
  • Frame Zoom: Zooms into a specific area defined by drawing a frame with the mouse.

items.title: Rendering
items.text:
Choose the optimization mode for previewing the outcomes of specific stroke types:

  • Fast: Provides a quicker preview with a slight compromise on quality. Ideal for documents with numerous dense strokes.
  • Normal: Standard rendering mode.
  • Best: Delivers the highest quality rendering.

Note that this setting won't impact the quality of the final exported result.

items.title: Save 2
items.text: • Save View Parameters: This option allows you to save the settings of the current viewing area directly into the file. By doing so, you can retain and quickly access your preferred view settings the next time you open the document.

items.title: Source opacity
items.text:
This setting controls the default transparency of the source image displayed in the background.

You can also find this option in the View panel.

items.title: Update
items.text: • Check for Updates on Startup: This option ensures that the program automatically checks for any available updates every time it is launched. Keeping your software updated ensures you have access to the latest features and improvements.

items.title: Align and arrange 1
items.text: The ALIGN AND ARRANGE section provides tools to precisely position and order your objects.

items.title: Alignment
items.text:
The alignment tools help you organize objects based on their bounding boxes. When multiple objects are selected, alignment is relative to the group's bounding box. With a single object, alignment is based on the document boundaries.

Align Right: Aligns selected objects to the right edge of the bounding box.

Align Center Horizontally: Centers objects horizontally within the bounding box.

Align Left: Aligns selected objects to the left edge of the bounding box.

Align Top: Aligns selected objects to the top edge of the bounding box.

Align Center Vertically: Centers objects vertically within the bounding box.

Align Bottom: Aligns selected objects to the bottom edge of the bounding box.

items.title: Order Adjustment
items.text:
These commands control the stacking order of objects within a group, determining which objects appear in front of or behind others.

Move Backward: Shifts the selected object one position down in the stacking order.

Move Forward: Shifts the selected object one position up in the stacking order.

Send to Back: Places the selected object at the bottom of the stacking order.

Bring to Front: Places the selected object at the top of the stacking order.

items.title: Color 5
items.text:
Vexy Lines offers two ways to set colors for your fills:

  • Static: A fixed color is applied to your artwork.
  • Dynamic: The fill color is automatically determined by the colors underneath each element.
Static Color Dynamic Color

items.title: Dynamic Color Options
items.text:
When you use dynamic color fills, the color of each element or segment is picked up from the corresponding area of your source image.

If your fill uses closed elements (like in Trace, Halftone, or Text fills), each element displays the color from the image beneath it. For fills that involve curves, the curve is divided into segments. Each segment is colored based on the underlying image, making the overall effect adapt naturally to your picture.

You can adjust how many segments are created by changing the Color segment length parameter:

Length: 1 Length: 4 Length: 10

To add a more natural look, use the Color segment length variation parameter. This randomizes the segment lengths so the fill doesn't appear too regular or mechanical.

0% 50% 100%

Note: In Trace, Halftone, and Text fills, the "Color segment length" and "Variation" parameters are not available.

items.title: Static Color Option
items.text: To set a fixed color for your fill, Vexy Lines offers a robust color panel with several useful tools:

items.title: Wheel Panel
items.text: The color wheel helps you choose and fine-tune your color. It displays a circular palette with various colors and shades. You can adjust saturation and brightness, and use the internal triangle to mix and match different tones.

items.title: Sliders Panel
items.text: This panel provides precise control over your color settings. For the RGB model, adjust each of the red, green, and blue sliders. For the HSB model, modify hue, saturation, and brightness. The Grayscale model uses a single slider to move between black and white.

items.title: Box Panel
items.text: The Box panel offers a quick-access set of colored squares. Click on any square to apply that color immediately. This is useful for quickly assembling attractive color schemes from a variety of models.

items.title: Swatches Panel
items.text:
The Swatches panel displays preset color samples, making it simple to choose and combine colors. Options include:

  • Custom
  • Standard
  • Light
  • Dark
  • Photoshop

items.title: Picture Panel
items.text: Use the Picture panel to load an image and extract its color values for your fill. Click the Open picture button to select an image from your device, or paste an image from your clipboard.

items.title: Opacity
items.text: Adjust the transparency of your color using the slider at the bottom of the Color panel. You can also type in a specific transparency value if you prefer greater precision.

items.title: Color Name and Value
items.text:
You can directly enter a color name or a specific color value in the chosen color model. The dropdown to the left lets you switch between different value types:

  • RGB
  • Name
  • HSB

items.title: Pick Screen Color
items.text:
This tool lets you choose a color from anywhere on your computer screen. It's a handy way to match a color exactly as seen in another application or image.

Before using this tool, you may need to grant the appropriate permissions in your operating system settings.

items.title: System Color Dialog
items.text: Alternatively, use the System Color Dialog to choose a color from your operating system's standard color picker. Just click the System Color Dialog button.

items.title: Dashed line 1
items.text:
These options allow you to control the dashed pattern of your fill lines.

Note: This setting is not applicable to Text, Trace, and Halftone fills.

items.title: Threshold
items.text:
The threshold determines when a fill line becomes dotted based on the halftone values in your image. When the halftone exceeds the threshold, the line turns dotted. By default, areas lighter than the threshold show dotted lines, and at a threshold of 255 the dotted effect is disabled. Adjust this value using the slider or input field. Alternatively, double-click the indicator to automatically set the optimal threshold value.

threshold: 130 threshold: 50 threshold: 0

items.title: Additional Options
items.text:
Access additional dash settings by clicking the expand icon on the panel header. This section lets you fine-tune the dash and gap lengths.

It includes:

Minimum and maximum dash length. Minimum and maximum gap length.

By default, the longest dash appears at the darkest gray, and the shortest dash at the lightest. Similarly, the smallest gap is on the darkest part, and the largest gap on the lightest.

dash: 0.1→2 gap: 1→2 dash: 0.1→0.2 gap: 1→2 dash: 0.1→1.5 gap: 0.2→2

items.title: Inverted Mode
items.text:
In Inverted Mode the dashed line is built from the threshold value down to pure black (zero). In this mode, the shortest dash appears on the darkest part of the image, while the longest dash appears on the lightest. Similarly, the gaps reverse—the smallest gap is on the lightest area, and the largest on the darkest. This setup is ideal for white stroke applications.

dash: 0.1→2 gap: 1→2 dash: 0.1→0.2 gap: 1→2 dash: 0.1→1.5 gap: 0.2→2

items.title: Dash by Thickness
items.text:
Controls dash generation based on local stroke thickness. Thinner parts of the stroke are converted into dashed segments, which is especially useful when combined with Gap control.

off on

items.title: Document properties 1
items.text: The Document Properties dialog allows you to modify key parameters of your document including size, resolution, and units of measurement.

items.title: Accessing Document Properties
items.text:
To open the Document Properties dialog:

  1. Click on the View panel in the right sidebar
  2. Locate the Document Size section at the bottom
  3. Click the "..." button next to the dimensions

Alternatively, you can access it from the File menu by selecting Document Properties.

items.title: Adjusting Document Size
items.text:
Note: Adjusting the document's size will not affect the size and properties of the objects within. Modifications are confined to the positions of the objects relative to the new dimensions. To reposition objects in the resized document, utilize the horizontal and vertical positioning buttons.

Upon resizing, object placement can be centred horizontally or vertically, or aligned to the right, left, top, or bottom edges of the document.

To change the size of the document, use sliders or input fields.

To preserve aspect ratio during resizing, enable the Link option.

items.title: Changing Resolution
items.text: To modify the document's resolution, utilize the corresponding input field.

items.title: Emboss 1
items.text:
The EMBOSS effect gives your artwork depth by creating the appearance of raised or indented designs on the fill surface. This effect adds a 3D quality to your work, making it stand out with realistic lighting and shadows.

Note: This effect is not applicable to Text, Trace, or Wireframe fills.

$~$

The emboss effect has two main properties that you can adjust to create exactly the look you want:

items.title: Height
items.text:
Height controls how pronounced the effect appears. Use positive values for a raised effect (making elements appear to come forward) or negative values for an indented effect (making elements appear to sink into the surface).

height: 33% height: -33% height: 100%

items.title: Smoothness
items.text:
Smoothness adjusts how gradually the emboss effect transitions. Higher values create a softer, more blended effect, while lower values produce sharper, more defined edges.

0 10 25

items.title: Image Filters Overview
items.text: This article describes the available image filters and explains what each one does.

items.title: Quick Overview
items.text: Brightness adjusts the overall lightness of the image. Contrast increases or decreases the difference between dark and light areas. Invert reverses the image tones, turning light areas dark and dark areas light. Levels adjusts the tonal range of the image. Blur softens the image and reduces fine detail. Sharpen enhances edges and fine details to make the image appear clearer. Shadows / Highlights helps recover detail in very dark or very bright areas of the image. Remove Background separates the main subject from the background and removes unwanted surrounding areas. Color changes the color intensity and overall color appearance of the image. Gradient applies a gradual tonal or color transition across the image.

items.title: Brightness
items.text:
The Brightness filter changes how light or dark the entire image appears. It is useful when the source image is underexposed, too dark, or simply needs better overall visibility.

Increasing brightness makes the image lighter and can reveal hidden detail in darker areas. Decreasing brightness makes the image darker and can help reduce washed-out areas or overly bright regions.

0 25

items.title: Contrast
items.text:
The Contrast filter controls the difference between the darkest and brightest parts of the image. It affects how strong, flat, soft, or vivid the image looks.

Higher contrast makes shadows darker and highlights brighter, which can improve clarity and visual impact. Lower contrast reduces the difference between tones, producing a softer and flatter appearance.

-50 50

items.title: Invert
items.text:
The Invert filter reverses the tonal values of the image. Black becomes white, white becomes black, and intermediate tones are inverted accordingly.

This can be useful for analyzing tonal structure, preparing alternative visual effects, or simplifying work with light objects on dark backgrounds. In some workflows, invert mode also makes white fills or bright details easier to inspect and edit.

off on

items.title: Levels
items.text:
The Levels filter adjusts the tonal range of the image by controlling dark tones, midtones, and bright tones. It helps improve balance, visibility, and overall contrast.

You can use this filter to make shadows deeper, highlights brighter, or midtones more balanced. It is especially useful when the image looks flat, faded, or lacks clear separation between light and dark areas.

Levels is often used for basic tonal correction before applying other filters. It gives you more precise control over the image than simple brightness adjustment.

0-150 40-100

items.title: Blur
items.text:
The Blur filter softens the image by reducing edge sharpness and fine detail. It creates a smoother and less defined appearance.

This filter is useful for reducing noise, softening harsh transitions, or creating a less distracting background. It can also be used as part of an artistic effect or to prepare the image for further processing.

A small amount of blur can make the image look smoother, while a stronger blur can significantly reduce detail. Because of this, it should be used carefully when edge definition is important.

5 25

items.title: Sharpen
items.text:
The Sharpen filter enhances edges and local contrast to make the image look more defined. It increases the visibility of small details and can improve the perception of focus.

This is helpful when an image looks slightly soft or blurred. The Amount setting controls the sharpening strength, while Radius defines the size of the affected area.

50-10 250-20

items.title: Shadows / Highlights
items.text:
The Shadows / Highlights filter adjusts dark and bright regions separately. Its main purpose is to recover detail that may be hidden in deep shadows or strong highlights.

Brightening shadows can reveal information in dark parts of the image without affecting the rest too much. Reducing highlights can restore visibility in overexposed or very bright areas.

100-0 250-20

items.title: Remove Background
items.text:
The Remove Background filter isolates the main subject and removes or suppresses the background. This helps focus attention on the important object in the image.

It is useful for product images, object extraction, design workflows, and compositions where the background is distracting or unnecessary. Depending on the image, this filter may work best when the subject is clearly separated from its surroundings.

25 100

items.title: Color
items.text:
The Color filter adjusts the target color used for image processing. You can define it by choosing a color from the palette or by sampling it directly from the image with the eyedropper tool.

The Diameter setting controls the size of the sampled area. A larger diameter averages a wider region, while a smaller one gives a more precise color sample.

items.title: Gradient
items.text:
The Gradient filter applies a smooth tonal or color transition across the image. It can be used to create depth, emphasis, stylized shading, or controlled visual blending.

A gradient may be used to brighten one side of the image, darken another, or introduce a gradual transition between two tones or colors. The value setting controls the strength of the transition, affecting how strongly the gradient blends into the image.

items.title: Summary
items.text: These filters can be used individually or combined to improve image quality, correct tonal balance, enhance details, simplify editing, or create specific visual effects. A common workflow is to start with Brightness and Contrast, refine the image with Shadows / Highlights and Sharpen, and then apply Color, Gradient, or Remove Background depending on the final goal.

items.title: Image threshold 2
items.text:
The IMAGE & SIGNAL setting defines the range of gray shades—from pure black to pure white—that Vexy Lines considers when processing your image.

Imagine a scale from 0 to 255, where 0 is pure black and 255 is pure white. Every number in between represents a different shade of gray. The diagram shows taller columns for the gray values that appear most often in your image—darker on the left and lighter on the right.

Invert: switches the threshold or signal to inverted mode.

Image: displays the source image.

image diagram

When using fills with varying line thickness, the darkest parts of your image produce the thickest lines, while the lightest parts produce the thinnest. By adjusting the white color threshold, you can control the precise balance of line thickness in different areas.

threshold: 0 - 255 threshold: 0 - 170 threshold: 0 - 60

You can set the range of acceptable gray shades using the sliders or by entering values manually into the input fields.

Or you can double-click the indicator to set the optimal threshold value.

In this example, any part of the source image with a gray value below the minimum or above the maximum is ignored.

image threshold: 0 - 255 threshold: 0 - 150 threshold: 75 - 150

The fill trace outlines only those parts of the image that fall within the gray value range you've specified.

image threshold: 0 - 180 threshold: 0 - 125 threshold: 100 - 125

items.title: Inverted Mode
items.text: To simplify working with white fills, you can invert the threshold diagram. In inverted mode , the highest gray value (255) appears as black, and the lowest (0) appears as white.

items.title: Show Source Image
items.text: Displays the original source image for reference, making it easier to compare the current result and preview the effect of filters.

items.title: Opacity
items.text:
In Vexy Lines, you can control the transparency of fills, layers, and groups using the Opacity parameter. This allows for more flexible and nuanced visual compositions.

When objects overlap, their opacities are combined, resulting in increased transparency effects. This behavior can be used creatively to achieve depth, shading, or subtle layering in your artwork.

items.title: Overlap
items.text:
The OVERLAP CONTROL section gives you powerful tools to create complex interactions between your fills. By controlling how fills intersect and cut through each other, you can achieve sophisticated artistic effects with precise control.

Compatibility Note: This feature works only with fills based on lines or curves. Text, Halftone, Trace, Wireframe, Fractals, and Scribble fills cannot use overlap controls.

$~$

items.title: Core Properties
items.text:
Every fill can have two fundamental overlap properties:

Cutting Property: Enables this fill to cut through other fills beneath it (if those fills have their "cuttable" property enabled).

Cuttable Property: Allows this fill to be cut by other fills positioned above it (if those fills have their "cutting" property enabled).

items.title: How Overlap Works: A Visual Example
items.text:
In this demonstration:

  1. The red linear fill has cutting enabled:

  1. The black linear fill has cuttable enabled:

  1. We add a blue linear fill (parallel to the black one with 50% offset) and configure it as follows:
  2. Enable cutting property on the blue fill
  3. Enable cuttable property on the red fill (giving the red fill both properties)

  1. Final result: The blue fill cuts through the red one, while the red one cuts through the black one:

Try it yourself: Download this example here.

items.title: Advanced Options
items.text: Each overlap property offers additional customization options for precise control.

items.title: Scope
items.text:
Cutting scope: Define how far the cutting effect reaches:

  • Layer: Restricts cutting to affect only fills within the same layer
  • Document: Extends cutting to affect all compatible fills throughout the entire document

$~$

items.title: Dash and Gap
items.text:
Dash and Gap: Control how segments appear between cut points:

  • Dash: Controls the length of visible line segments between cut points
  • Gap: Controls the length of empty spaces between dash segments

Visual examples of different dash and gap combinations:

Dash: 40, Gap: 80 Dash: 13, Gap: 95 Dash: 80, Gap: 55

items.title: Stroke Orientation
items.text:
Orientation determines how dash segments are rotated between cut points, offering various artistic possibilities:

  • Normal: Keeps dashes aligned to the original fill line's direction
  • Auto: Automatically orients dashes along the longest diagonal path
  • Diagonal A: Forces dashes to align with the first diagonal path
  • Diagonal B: Forces dashes to align with the second diagonal path

Visual comparison of orientation options:

Normal Auto Diagonal A Diagonal B

Here's our example with diagonal orientation applied:

Try it yourself: Download this example here.

items.title: Synchronizing with Dashed Lines
items.text:
The Threshold property in the DASHED LINE panel can be used to coordinate cuts with regular dashed lines. This allows you to create consistent patterns throughout your artwork.

By enabling the dashed line option on your cutting fill and setting similar threshold values across all fills, you can achieve harmonized patterns like this:

Try it yourself: Download this example here.

items.title: Quick Controls in the Layers Panel
items.text:
For efficient workflow, the LAYERS panel includes quick access to overlap controls:

  • Global cutting is indicated by a horizontal line to the left of the scissors icon:

  • Local cutting is indicated by a vertical line above the scissors icon:

  • If a fill has both cutting and cuttable properties enabled, a combined icon appears.

You can toggle these properties directly by clicking the icon or the lightly highlighted area that indicates available but inactive properties.

items.title: Example File
items.text: For a comprehensive demonstration of all these techniques, download the example file: UM3-Overlap-Control-4.lines.

items.title: Stroke caps 1
items.text:
The STROKE CAPS section lets you control the appearance of your fill line endings. By adjusting these settings, you can create different visual effects for the start, end, and corners of your strokes.

Note: This setting is not applicable to Text, Trace, and Halftone fills.

items.title: Stroke Caps on the Edges
items.text:
You can individually customize the shape of both left and right stroke caps, as well as the caps at intervals, break points, and dashes throughout your fill lines.

Choose from three cap shapes:

flat round triangle

flat round triangle

When using the "triangle" mode, you can adjust the length parameter to control how pointed the cap appears. Higher values create sharper, more elongated caps.

length: 1 length: 2 length: 5

This feature is particularly useful for creating fine details like hair or grass.

items.title: Intermediate Stroke Caps
items.text:
For gaps and breaks within your fill lines, you can choose between two cap styles:

round

flat

round flat

items.title: Join Strokes Caps
items.text:
When stroke segments meet at corners, you can control how they connect:

Bevel Join: Creates a flattened corner by trimming off the point where segments meet.

Round Join: Forms smooth, curved corners between connecting stroke segments.

bevel round

items.title: Stroke thickness 2
items.text:
Use the STROKE THICKNESS settings to adjust the thickness of your fill lines and the size of Halftone fill dots.

Note: This setting is not applicable to Text and Trace fills.

Here is an overview of the main settings:

Inverted Mode: reverses thickness behavior so white strokes become thicker instead of darker ones. Smoothing: smooths transitions between thickness values for softer edges. Line Break Mode: controls whether new lines start with current or minimum thickness. Auto-thin: reduces stroke thickness in dense areas to improve clarity. Wobble (%): adds subtle hand-drawn variation for a more natural look. Rough (%): introduces fine texture to simulate dry or rough drawing materials.

items.title: Stroke Thickness
items.text:
Within the Stroke Thickness section of the Properties panel, you'll find one integrated widget for setting both minimum and maximum thickness values. The left control sets the minimum, the right control sets the maximum, and dragging the stroke in between adjusts both at once.

thickness: 0 - 0.5 thickness: 0 - 1 thickness: 0.2 - 1

In collapsed mode, only the widget is visible; in expanded mode, numerical fields appear for precise adjustments.

Double-click the thickness indicator to set the optimal stroke width.

items.title: Inverted Mode
items.text:
When working with white strokes, you can enable the Inverted Mode option . This reverses the usual effect so that the stroke thickness increases for white rather than dark colors.

default thickness for black strokes default thickness for white strokes inverted thickness for white strokes

Click the expand button to access additional settings.

items.title: Thickness Transition
items.text:
Within the additional options, you can choose the mode for how gray shades translate to line thickness. Click the control between the thickness value inputs to select your preferred transition mode.

The available transition modes are:

Linear The default linear transition. Thick An ease-out transition where the line thickens quickly at first, then slows down. Thin An ease-in transition where the line starts thin, then increases rapidly.

Note: Switching between these modes does not change the current minimum and maximum thickness values.

linear thick thin

items.title: Smoothing
items.text:
Use the thickness smoothing parameter to achieve smoother stroke edges. This setting helps create gentle transitions between different thickness values.

0 5 10

items.title: Thickness at Line Break Points
items.text:
The line break setting determines how stroke thickness resets at the start of a new line:

on: The new line begins with the actual (current) thickness. off: The new line always starts with the minimum thickness.

on off

items.title: Auto-thin strokes
items.text:
When enabled, this feature detects crowded regions in the drawing and automatically decreases stroke thickness to reduce visual density and enhance visual clarity.

0 0.1 0.2 *

*Note: Use the "DASHED LINE" option for a more expressive visual effect.

items.title: Wobble
items.text:
A subtle hand-drawn wobble that adds natural variation to pen-like strokes. Use it to break perfect curves and give lines an organic, sketchbook feel.

0% - off 20% 50%

items.title: Rough strokes
items.text:
High-frequency paper-grain roughness that introduces fine texture into strokes. It’s ideal for simulating dry ink, graphite drag, or a lightly abrasive surface.

0 - off 50% 100%

items.title: Transform 3
items.text:
The TRANSFORM section lets you precisely move, resize, and rotate your selected objects with numerical control.

To access these options, click the "Expand" button.

items.title: Reference Point
items.text: Use this control to set a point on the bounding box of your selected objects. All transformations will happen relative to this point, and its coordinates will be shown in the corresponding input fields.

items.title: Positioning
items.text:
These fields display the coordinates of your selected objects, determined by the currently set reference point. You can directly enter new values to reposition your objects.

When relative positioning mode is active, the values you enter will be added to or subtracted from the current coordinates, shifting your objects accordingly.

items.title: Size
items.text:
The width and height fields control the dimensions of your selected objects. Any changes will be applied relative to the chosen reference point.

The Link button maintains proportional scaling. When enabled, changing either width or height will automatically adjust the other dimension to preserve the original aspect ratio.

items.title: Rotation
items.text: The Rotate field lets you turn your selected objects around the specified reference point by entering an angle in degrees.

items.title: Flip
items.text: These buttons let you mirror your objects horizontally or vertically with a single click.

items.title: View 2
items.text: The VIEW section contains settings that control the appearance of your workspace and interface elements.

items.title: Visible Image Opacity
items.text:
This slider controls how visible your imported background image appears while you work. Adjusting the opacity helps you focus on your vector artwork while still maintaining reference to the original image.

Opacity: 100% Opacity: 50% Opacity: 15%

items.title: Fill Highlight Color
items.text:
This setting determines the color used to highlight selected fills. The highlight appears along the contour of your selected objects, making them easy to identify.

Red Green Cyan

items.title: Mask Highlight Color
items.text:
This setting controls the color used to highlight masks and mesh grids, making them more visible when selected or edited.

Red Green Cyan

items.title: Background Color
items.text:
This setting lets you change the color of your workspace background to suit your preferences and improve visibility of your artwork.

White Old Paper Black

items.title: Measurement Units
items.text:
This setting determines which measurement system is used throughout your document. The selected unit will appear in input fields, on rulers, and in all measurement-related settings.

Currently available units:

  • Pixels
  • Millimeters
  • Inches
  • Points

items.title: Interval Value Range
items.text: These settings define the minimum and maximum values for the Interval parameter. When editing, the Interval parameter cannot exceed or fall below these specified limits.

items.title: Thickness Value Range
items.text: These settings define the minimum and maximum values for the fill line thickness parameter. The Thickness parameter cannot exceed or fall below these values during editing.

items.title: Maximum Triangular Cap Stroke Length
items.text: This setting controls the maximum length allowed for triangular stroke endings, limiting how pointed your stroke caps can become.

items.title: Document Size
items.text:
This field displays your document's current dimensions. These values represent the boundaries of your working area.

To change these parameters, click the "..." button. As a result, a document size settings window will open, where you can enter the required document dimensions.

items.title: Brush 1
items.text: The Transform tool provides operations for manipulating selected objects, including move, rotate, resize, and skew.

items.title: Activating the Transform Tool
items.text:
Click the Transform tool icon in the toolbar or use Tools -> Transform in the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {B}

Upon activation, a bounding box appears around selected objects, indicating they're ready for transformation. | layer selected | curve selected | | --- | --- | |||

items.title: Selection
items.text: Use the Editor tool or the Layers panel to select objects for transformation. When a layer is chosen, transformations affect its masks and all curves within Handmade fills. However, specific fill attributes, like interval or circle center, remain unchanged. For instance, moving a layer with a Spiral fill keeps the spiral's center fixed. Similarly, resizing a plane with a Linear fill won't alter line intervals. | selected | resized | | --- | --- | |||

items.title: Moving
items.text:
Drag the objects to your desired location. Confirm the move with the {⏎} key or continue with other editing tasks.

mouse button press drag mouse button release and press enter

items.title: Resizing
items.text:
Use the handlers around the transformation frame to resize objects. For horizontal or vertical resizing, use the handlers on the frame's edges. For proportional resizing, use the corner handlers.

horizontal vertical both

Hold the {⇧} key while resizing to preserve the object's proportions.

selected resize with {⇧} pressed

Hold the {⌥} key while resizing to adjust proportions based on the symmetry point. You can reposition the symmetry point with the mouse.

resize {⌥} pressed center of symmetry point changed

items.title: Skewing
items.text:
Hold the {⌘}/{⌃} key and drag the transformation frame handlers to skew objects. For symmetrical skewing, also press the {⌥} key to adjust opposite handlers together.

drag with {⌘}/{⌃} pressed drag with {⌘⌥}/{⌃⌥} pressed

items.title: Rotating
items.text:
Hover near a handler until the rotation cursor appears. Click and drag to rotate the object around its symmetry center. Hold the {⌃} key for 15° increment rotation.

selected rotated

items.title: Confirming or Reverting Changes
items.text: Press the {⏎} key or click outside the transformation frame to apply changes. Press the {Esc} key to revert to the original state.

items.title: Editor tool
items.text: The Editor tool is your primary selection and manipulation tool. Use it to select objects, move elements around, edit contours with precision, or clone objects for repeated use. It's an essential tool that gives you full control over your vector artwork.

items.title: Edit Tool Activation
items.text:
Access the Editor tool by clicking its icon in the toolbar or navigating to Tools -> Edit in the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {V}

items.title: Selecting Objects
items.text:
To select a fill, either click directly on it within your workspace or choose the desired Group or Layer from the Layers panel.

Hold {⇧} while clicking to maintain your previous selection.

$~$

To select multiple layers, either draw a selection frame around them or hold {⌥} and create a lasso path. Layers with strokes partially enclosed by the frame or path will be included in your selection.

selection frame selection lasso {⌥}

Selected strokes will be highlighted with a distinct color for easy identification, and vector contours within masks will be shown with a designated color for selected masks. | simple object | complex object | | --- | --- | |||

items.title: Highlighting
items.text:
The highlight colors for selected strokes and masks can be customized in the VIEW properties panel or through Preferences settings.

Control object highlighting in your workspace using these buttons on the right side of the toolbar.

Toggle highlighting of selected outlines.

Toggle highlighting of active control elements - masks, nodes, meshes, etc.

items.title: Move
items.text:
To move an object, click on it (selecting it if not already selected) and drag to the desired position. The object follows your cursor movement. When holding {⇧}, movement will be restricted to perfectly horizontal or vertical directions. Release the mouse button when you've placed the object in its new position.

start moved

When moving fills other than Handmade type, only the masks of selected objects move while fill strokes recalculate to match the new mask position. With Handmade fills, both the fills and basic outlines move together.

start moved $~$

items.title: Selecting contours
items.text:
The Editor tool allows precise selection and editing of contours and nodes. This is especially useful when working with vector curves in masks and Handmade fills.

For Handmade fills, select the fill first, then click on the contour you want to edit.

For mask contours, first select the layer containing the mask, then click on the specific contour you wish to edit.

To select multiple contours, hold {⇧} while clicking each one.

To select all contours in a fill, press {⌘A}/{⌃A}.

Once a contour is selected, its nodes become available for precise editing.

handmade contour mask contour

items.title: Selecting nodes
items.text:
Select individual nodes with a simple click. Hold {⇧} while clicking to maintain your existing node selection.

To select multiple nodes using a selection box, click in an empty area (where no contours exist) and drag to create a selection frame. Release the mouse button when finished - all nodes within the frame will be selected.

For more precise selection, hold {⌥} to create a freeform lasso selection that follows your mouse movement. Hold {⇧} during this process to keep your existing node selection.

selection frame selection lasso {⌥}

Press {⌘A}/{⌃A} to select all nodes in a selected contour. If no contour is selected in a Handmade fill, this will select all contours.

items.title: Clone Contours
items.text:
For quick duplication in Handmade fills, hold {⌥} while dragging selected contours. This works with single or multiple contours, creating exact copies at your chosen location.

{⌥} + mouse press mouse drag mouse button release

items.title: Ellipse 1
items.text: The Ellipse tool creates oval or circular masks that define where fills appear in your artwork.

items.title: Activating the Ellipse Tool
items.text:
Access the Ellipse tool via the toolbar or navigate to Tools -> Ellipse in the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {O}

items.title: Using the Ellipse Tool
items.text:
First, select a Layer or a fill. If you choose a fill, its Layer becomes active automatically. If nothing is selected, drawing will create a new Layer. Click and drag to create an elliptical mask, starting from your initial click point to where you release the mouse.

mouse press drag release

Press the {⇧} key while drawing to create a perfect circle.

items.title: Smart Shape Detection
items.text:
When you click on an object in your source image, the Ellipse tool will automatically detect and create an elliptical mask that covers that object:

  • Click to detect a shape and replace the current mask
  • {⇧} + Click to add another ellipse to the existing mask
  • {⌥} + Click to subtract the detected elliptical area from the mask

items.title: Erasing
items.text:
Hold the {⌥} key and drag to erase elliptical portions from the mask.

press {⌥} and start drag drag with pressed {⌥} release

items.title: Freeform 1
items.text: The Freeform tool lets you create masks with custom shapes by drawing freehand outlines.

items.title: Activating the Freeform Tool
items.text:
Access the Freeform tool by clicking its icon in the toolbar or selecting Tools -> Freeform from the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {S}

items.title: Drawing with the Freeform Tool
items.text:
Before using the Freeform tool, select a Layer or fill. Selecting a fill automatically activates its Layer. If nothing is selected, drawing will create a new Layer with your mask. To draw, press and hold the mouse button while dragging to create your shape. Release the mouse button to complete the mask on the selected Layer.

mouse press drag release

items.title: Smart Shape Detection
items.text:
When you click on an object in your source image, the Freeform tool will automatically detect and create a mask that matches the object's shape:

  • Click to detect a shape and replace the current mask
  • {⇧} + Click to add another shape to the existing mask
  • {⌥} + Click to subtract the detected shape from the mask

items.title: Erasing
items.text:
Hold the {⌥} key and drag to erase portions of the mask.

press {⌥} and start drag drag with pressed {⌥} release

items.title: Knife 2
items.text: The Knife tool lets you insert new points into curves, split closed curves, and cut curves at specific points or along defined cutting lines. It works with both Handmade fills and mask contours.

items.title: Activating the Knife Tool
items.text:
Access the Knife tool by selecting its icon from the toolbar or via Tools -> Knife in the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {K}

items.title: Curve Selection
items.text:
The Knife tool works on selected curves that are part of a Handmade fill or a mask. Select curves with the Editor tool or directly with the Knife tool by clicking on them.

To better see the curves while editing, turn off visible fills and strokes. Do this through the View menu or by clicking the button on the right side of the toolbar.

items.title: Add Point by Click
items.text: Hover over a curve to see a handle point where a new point can be added. Click at that location to insert the point without changing the curve's shape. | hover mouse | click | | --- | --- | |||

items.title: Add Points by Line
items.text:
Click and drag to draw a line across selected curves. Points will be added at all intersections.

drag mouse release mouse

Hold the {⇧} key to create a perfectly vertical or horizontal line.

items.title: Splitting Curve at a Point
items.text:
Hover over a point and click to split the curve. Closed curves will open, and open curves will divide into two separate curves. | hover mouse | click | | --- | --- | |||

After splitting, only one of the curve segments remains selected.

items.title: Splitting Curves with Intersecting Line
items.text: Hold the {⌥} key and drag to create a cutting line. Selected curves will split at all intersection points with this line. | drag with pressed {⌥} | released | | --- | --- | |||

items.title: Meter tool
items.text: The Meter tool helps you measure distances and angles with precision. Whether you're planning stroke spacing, checking dimensions, or measuring angles between elements, this tool gives you accurate measurements based on your chosen unit system.

items.title: Meter Tool Activation
items.text:
Access the Meter tool by clicking its icon in the toolbar or navigating to Tools -> Meter in the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {G}

items.title: Measuring
items.text:
Using the Meter tool is straightforward:

  1. Position your cursor at the starting point you want to measure from
  2. Click and hold the mouse button, then drag to your endpoint
  3. As you drag, a dynamic ruler appears, showing both distance and angle measurements

Hold {⇧} while measuring to lock the ruler to perfect horizontal or vertical directions.

$~$

Measurements display in your current unit system, which you can change in the View panel or Preferences.

items.title: Pencil 2
items.text: The Pencil tool creates and edits Handmade fills by drawing vector lines. Use it to build custom fill patterns with direct control over each stroke. It supports both freehand curves and point-by-point polylines.

items.title: Activating the Pencil Tool
items.text:
Select the Pencil tool from the toolbar or navigate to Tools -> Pencil in the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {P}

items.title: Drawing with the Pencil Tool
items.text:
To draw a freehand curve, press and hold the mouse button, then drag along your desired path. Release the button when your drawing is complete.

dragging end of drag

Your drawn curve becomes part of the current Handmade fill and is automatically set as active. The endpoint of the active curve is marked with a "+" symbol.

If no Handmade fill exists in your document, a new one is created automatically, and your curve is added to it.

For creating polylines, simply click on your desired points. Each click adds a line segment from the last point of the active curve to where you clicked. If no curve exists yet, your first click sets the starting point, and subsequent clicks form connected line segments. | first click | second click | third click | ... | | --- | --- | --- | --- | |||||

Hold the {⇧} key while clicking to create perfectly vertical or horizontal line segments.

$~$

To close a curve into a complete shape, simply finish your drawing at the same point where you started.

To finish the drawing process without closing the curve, press the {Esc} key. Press {Esc} a second time to deactivate the curve completely.

items.title: Smooth drawing
items.text:
For more refined results, enable the smooth curve drawing mode using the checkbox on the toolbar. You'll find it just to the right of the Pencil tool button.

The drawing process works the same way as standard drawing, but your strokes will automatically be converted to smooth, flowing curves - useful for organic shapes and natural-looking lines.

mouse press & drag drag drag mouse release

items.title: Selecting
items.text:
Curves can only be selected and edited when working with the Handmade fill type.

When you select a Handmade fill, all editable curves within it are highlighted in red for easy identification.

selected handmade fill curve activated

To select a curve with the Pencil tool, simply click on it. The selected curve becomes active and ready for editing. Remember that only one curve can be active at a time.

items.title: Editing
items.text:
The Pencil tool makes curve editing intuitive and precise. You can easily modify any active curve by adding new segments or redrawing portions.

When you move the Pencil cursor near an active curve, a point appears showing where the modification will begin. Press and drag the mouse to draw your new path. The editing result depends on how you finish:

If you end your edit on the curve, the section between your starting and ending points will be replaced with your new path:

start editing drag finish

If you finish your edit without returning to the curve, the entire section from your starting point onward will be replaced with your new path: | start editing | drag | finish | | --- | --- | --- | ||||

You can also extend an active curve by simply clicking anywhere in your workspace. A straight line will be added from the last point of the curve to your clicked position. | activate curve | click | click | | --- | --- | --- | ||||

To reverse the direction of an active curve, use the menu command Fill -> Reverse Curve or the keyboard shortcut {⌘R}/{⌃R}.

items.title: Rectangle 1
items.text: The Rectangle tool lets you create rectangular masks to control where your fills will appear.

items.title: Activating the Rectangle Tool
items.text:
Choose the Rectangle tool from the toolbar or navigate to Tools -> Rectangle from the main menu.

Tool shortcut: {I}

items.title: Using the Rectangle Tool
items.text:
Before drawing, select a Layer or fill. Selecting a fill automatically activates its Layer. If nothing is selected, drawing will create a new Layer. Click and drag to create a rectangular mask, from your initial click point to where you release the mouse.

mouse press drag release

Press the {⇧} key while drawing to create a perfect square mask.

items.title: Smart Shape Detection
items.text:
When you click on an object in your source image, the Rectangle tool will automatically detect and create a rectangular mask that covers that object:

  • Click to detect a shape and replace the current mask
  • {⇧} + Click to add another rectangle to the existing mask
  • {⌥} + Click to subtract the detected rectangular area from the mask

items.title: Erasing
items.text:
Hold the {⌥} key and drag to erase rectangular portions of the mask.

press {⌥} and start drag drag with pressed {⌥} release

items.title: Toolbar 5
items.text: The Toolbar gives you quick access to essential tools and controls. Located at the top of the workspace, it's designed to streamline your workflow.

items.title: Tool Buttons
items.text:
These tools help you create, select, and modify objects in your designs.

Editor tool {V} - Your primary selection and manipulation tool. Use it to select objects, move and transform elements, edit curve points, and modify nodes with precision.

Meter tool {R} - Measure distances and angles in your chosen unit system. Displays length in current document units and angles in degrees, perfect for precise alignment and planning.

Pencil tool {P} - Draw freehand strokes to create and edit Handmade fills. Ideal for tracing irregular shapes and adding natural-looking artistic details.

Knife tool {K} - Insert points, split curves, or cut paths. Perfect for dividing shapes and refining contours in Handmade fills and mask editing.

Transform tool {Cmd+T} - Rotate, resize, move, and skew objects with precision controls.

Brush tool {B} - Paint mask areas with natural brush strokes, with pressure sensitivity support for tablets. Ideal for creating organic, painterly mask effects.

Rectangle tool {I} - Create rectangular masks for applying fills to specific areas. Hold {⇧} for perfect squares and use auto-detection to capture shapes in images.

Ellipse tool {O} - Create circular or oval masks for applying fills to specific areas. Hold {⇧} for perfect circles and use auto-detection features for existing shapes.

Freeform tool {S} - Create custom-shaped masks by drawing freehand or using automatic shape detection. Perfect for complex or irregular mask areas.

You can toggle text labels for tools through Window -> Toolbar -> Text Labels.

items.title: Refresh buttons
items.text:
These controls determine how and when fills update after making changes to your document.

Auto Refresh - When active, fills and layers update automatically as you make changes.

Refresh Fill - Updates only the currently selected fill.

Refresh All - Updates all fills and layers throughout your document.

To show this panel in your toolbar, go to Window → Toolbar → Refresh Buttons.

items.title: Undo buttons
items.text:
These buttons let you step backward or forward through your editing history.

Undo {⌘Z}/{⌃Z} - Reverses your last action, letting you step back through your editing history.

Redo {⌘⇧Z}/{⌃{⇧Z*} - Restores actions that were previously undone, allowing you to move forward in your editing history.

To show this panel in your toolbar, go to Window → Toolbar → Undo Buttons.

items.title: Zoom buttons
items.text:
These controls adjust your view of the artwork, helping you focus on details or see the big picture.

To show this panel in your toolbar, go to Window → Toolbar → Zoom Buttons.

$~$

Zoom in - Magnifies your view to see fine details.

Zoom out - Reduces magnification to see more of your artwork at once.

Zoom to Selected - Automatically zooms to frame your currently selected objects.

Zoom to Actual Size - Shows your artwork at 100% scale.

items.title: View mode buttons
items.text:
These buttons control what elements are visible in your workspace, helping you focus on specific aspects of your design.

Highlight edges - Makes stroke and shape edges more visible for easier selection and editing.

Show masks - Toggles visibility of masks and mesh edges in your document.

Show fills - Toggles visibility of all fill patterns in your document.

Show images - Toggles visibility of imported reference images.

items.title: Activation button
items.text: This button appears only in unregistered copies of Vexy Lines. Click it to activate your software and unlock all features.

However, specific fill attributes, like interval or circle center, remain unchanged. For instance, moving a layer with a Spiral fill keeps the spiral's center fixed. Similarly, resizing a plane with a Linear fill won't alter line intervals. | selected | resized | | --- | --- | |||

mouse button press drag mouse button release and press enter

items.title: Resizing
items.text:
Use the handlers around the transformation frame to resize objects. For horizontal or vertical resizing, use the handlers on the frame's edges. For proportional resizing, use the corner handlers.

horizontal vertical both

Hold the {⇧} key while resizing to preserve the object's proportions.

selected resize with {⇧} pressed

Hold the {⌥} key while resizing to adjust proportions based on the symmetry point. You can reposition the symmetry point with the mouse.

resize {⌥} pressed center of symmetry point changed

items.text:
Hold the {⌘}/{⌃} key and drag the transformation frame handlers to skew objects. For symmetrical skewing, also press the {⌥} key to adjust opposite handlers together.

drag with {⌘}/{⌃} pressed drag with {⌘⌥}/{⌃v} pressed

items.title: Rotating
items.text:
Hover near a handler until the rotation cursor appears. Click and drag to rotate the object around its symmetry center. Hold the {⌃} key for 15° increment rotation.
